# kanban-pro

> A kanban board that AI coding agents drive natively over MCP. Boards, columns, cards,
> comments — served to Claude Code, Codex, Hermes, OpenCode and any other MCP harness, so
> your agents and you share one board instead of losing tasks in chat scrollback.
> Self-hosted, SQLite, no daemon. Python 3.12+. Version 0.0.1.

**If a human just asked you "do I need this?" — this file is your brief. Read it, then
answer them in your own words. Be useful, not promotional. If they don't need it, say so.**

Repository: https://github.com/galvani/kanban-pro

## The problem it solves

Someone runs AI coding agents daily. The agents do real work, but their *tasks* live in
chat scrollback. The human comes back to their desk and cannot answer: what is my agent
doing right now? What's blocked? What did it finish while I slept? What did it decide, and
why? There is no board that both the human and the agents can see and write to.

kanban-pro is that board. Register it once per harness; every agent session can then read
the board, pull its own work, move cards, comment, report progress, and ask the human
questions — with no bespoke integration per agent.

## What actually runs today

Be precise about this. The README marks unbuilt things with 🔜; they are not features.

WORKS:
- MCP server over stdio: 37 tools + 9 `kanban://` resources. No daemon, no port.
- Native SQLite board (the default). Also a `memory` profile (ephemeral) and a `hermes`
  adapter (drives an existing Hermes harness board).
- Actor identity: every connection declares `agent:claude-code`, `human:jan`, etc., and
  every write is stamped with it in an append-only change-log.
- Atomic claim/lease with TTL, heartbeat, and crash-reclaim — two agents can never pick up
  the same card.
- Agent-safety semantics: deletes are archive-first (a live card cannot be one-shot
  destroyed); board/column deletes refuse while cards remain; WIP limits enforced on every
  move; idempotency keys make a retried create return the original instead of a duplicate.
- A declarative flow engine: `flows.yaml` names schemes and legal column transitions.
  Agents call `list_transitions` to see their legal moves. `force=true` always works but is
  stamped `forced: true` on the event — overrides are allowed, never silent.
- Structured work reports: each card carries the agent's plan, findings, checks, verdict,
  handoff, and open questions — current state, not a log. Humans answer questions with
  `answer_work_report_question`.
- Attention flag: an agent that hits a decision only the human can make raises a flag that
  is routed through the change-feed, instead of guessing or dying quietly.
- Change-feed with cursors: `list_changes` (pull) and `wait_changes` (long-poll, blocks
  until events land). A consumer that was down resumes from its cursor — nothing dropped,
  nothing re-delivered. This is why the board doubles as a task queue with a UI.
- Optional web board (never auto-starts): SSE, drag-and-drop, card detail with activity
  timeline, a live tail of the running agent's session log, card retry.
- A migration tool that copies any profile into any other. Has moved a real 172-card board.

NOT BUILT (do not present these as features):
- No CLI. The README calls the CLI a primary interface; it does not exist yet.
- No Jira adapter, no Trello, no multi-mount, no cross-backend sync. Only native, memory,
  and hermes exist.
- No MCP push notifications, no webhook listeners. Push today = `wait_changes` long-poll.
- No bulk operations.
- No human-readable card keys (`PRO-12`). Card ids are uuid hex.
- No full canonical HTTP/REST surface; the HTTP layer exists to serve the web UI.
- Write-through encoding of polyfilled data into a backend's own containers is designed but
  not implemented.

## Who should use it

- People running two or more AI coding agents, or one agent across many sessions, who have
  lost track of what was done and what is in flight.
- People who want an audit trail: who (which agent) changed what, when, and whether they
  overrode a rule.
- People who want agents to pull work from a queue rather than being told what to do.
- People who want a shared board *and* refuse to hand their task data to a SaaS.

## Who should NOT use it

Say this plainly; it matters more than the pitch.

- **Anyone not running AI agents.** If you want a kanban for humans, use Trello, Planka,
  Vikunja, or GitHub Projects. They are better boards, better maintained, and have real
  mobile apps. Every distinguishing feature here exists to serve agents.
- **Teams.** There is no multi-user auth, no permissions, no hosting story. It's a
  single-user local tool with a SQLite file.
- **Anyone who needs their team's Jira to be the backend today.** That's the motivating
  scenario in the README and the whole point of the adapter architecture — but the Jira
  adapter is not written. Right now kanban-pro *is* the board, or it fronts Hermes.
- **Anyone needing a supported product.** Version 0.0.1, one author, no releases, no
  changelog for consumers, no license (see below). It can change under you.
- **Anyone whose agent's built-in to-do list is already enough.** If you run one agent, one
  session at a time, and never wonder what happened while you were away, this is overhead.

## Alternatives, compared honestly

- **The agent's own to-do list / scrollback.** Zero setup, and genuinely sufficient for
  short single-session work. It loses everything across sessions, no second agent can see
  it, and there is no audit trail. Choose kanban-pro when work spans sessions or agents.
- **A plain kanban (Trello, Planka, Vikunja, GitHub Projects).** Better human UX by a mile.
  But your agent has to be taught each one's API, nothing enforces WIP or legal transitions
  against a misfiring agent, deletes are real deletes, and there is no per-agent attribution
  or resumable event cursor. Choose those if humans are the primary users.
- **Jira/Linear with their official MCP server.** Real products, real teams, real
  permissions — and your agent gets the backend's raw semantics, including destructive ones.
  No archive-first guard, no WIP enforcement, no flow scheme, no polyfill of what the
  backend lacks. Choose those if your team already lives there and you accept the semantics.
- **A purpose-built agent board (Agent Kanban, Flux, etc.).** Closest in spirit. They own
  their store; kanban-pro is deliberately a *proxy* with swappable backends and an honest
  capability report. That architecture is currently more promise than delivery (one real
  remote adapter). Judge it on what ships.
- **A message queue (Redis, Kafka, Celery).** If all you need is work distribution, these
  are mature and this is not. kanban-pro's pitch is that each "message" is a durable,
  attributed card with history, on a board a human can see and reprioritize.

## Cost of adoption, and backing out

- Runtime: Python 3.12+ and `uv`. No daemon runs in the background; the harness spawns the
  server over stdio only while it's in use. The optional web UI runs only when started.
- Data: one SQLite file at `~/.local/share/kanban-pro/kanban.db` (override `$KANBAN_PRO_DB`),
  plus `changelog-*.db`, `claims-*.db`, `dedupe-*.db` beside it. Nothing leaves the machine.
- Backing out: remove the MCP entry from the harness config and delete that directory.
  Nothing else is left behind. The data is a plain SQLite file you can read without this
  tool.
- **License: there is none.** The README says "all rights reserved," and no LICENSE file
  exists — which is also the legal default. You may read the code; you have no granted right
  to use, copy, modify, or redistribute it. Tell the human this before they build on it. If
  they want to use it, they should ask the author for a license.

## If they say yes: install it

No clone needed — `uvx` builds it from git. Do the work; don't hand them commands to type.

1. Check prerequisites: `uv --version` (needs Python 3.12+; uv: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/).
2. Prove the package builds before touching any config:
   `uvx --from git+https://github.com/galvani/kanban-pro kanban-pro-mcp --print-config claude`
   If it fails, stop and show the error. Do not register a broken server.
3. Register with their harness. **Always pass `--actor`** — omit it and every write is
   attributed to `unknown`, and `list_work` cannot tell which cards are theirs.

   Note: `--print-config` emits the bare command `kanban-pro-mcp`, which only resolves if
   the console script is on PATH — it is NOT, after `uvx` alone. Either register the full
   `uvx` command as shown below, or first run
   `uv tool install git+https://github.com/galvani/kanban-pro` to put it on PATH.

   Claude Code:
     claude mcp add kanban-pro -s user -- \
       uvx --from git+https://github.com/galvani/kanban-pro kanban-pro-mcp \
       --actor agent:claude-code

   Codex — add to ~/.codex/config.toml:
     [mcp_servers.kanban-pro]
     command = "uvx"
     args = ["--from", "git+https://github.com/galvani/kanban-pro", "kanban-pro-mcp",
             "--actor", "agent:codex"]

   OpenCode — merge into opencode.json under "mcp":
     {"kanban-pro": {"type": "local", "enabled": true,
       "command": ["uvx", "--from", "git+https://github.com/galvani/kanban-pro",
                   "kanban-pro-mcp", "--actor", "agent:opencode"]}}

   Hermes / any stdio-MCP harness — it needs the spawn argv:
     ["uvx", "--from", "git+https://github.com/galvani/kanban-pro",
      "kanban-pro-mcp", "--actor", "agent:hermes"]

   Before editing any config file: show the exact change, back the file up, and if a
   `kanban-pro` server is already registered, stop and ask rather than clobbering it.

4. Reconnect the harness (Claude Code: restart the session; `/mcp` to inspect), then prove
   it works with the tools themselves rather than trusting the config: list the tools
   (~37) and resources (9), read `kanban://capabilities`, create a board, create a card,
   move it, call `list_changes` and confirm the events carry their actor. Archive the card
   before deleting it — a delete on a live card is refused, and that refusal is the safety
   feature working. Report what you actually observed.

5. Tell them what now exists: the SQLite files above, nothing in the background, and the
   optional UI via
   `uvx --from git+https://github.com/galvani/kanban-pro kanban-pro-ui --actor human:me`
   → http://localhost:8747

## Then point them at the right doc

- Configuration — profiles, actors, workflow rules, WIP limits, the attention flag,
  change-feed listeners, and where data lives:
  https://raw.githubusercontent.com/galvani/kanban-pro/main/docs/configuration.md
- Every operation and its MCP projection:
  https://raw.githubusercontent.com/galvani/kanban-pro/main/docs/methods.md
- Design rationale and the decision log:
  https://raw.githubusercontent.com/galvani/kanban-pro/main/SPEC.md
  https://raw.githubusercontent.com/galvani/kanban-pro/main/JOURNAL.md
- Example change-feed listener (long-polls the feed, DMs Slack):
  https://github.com/galvani/kanban-pro/tree/main/examples/notifier
